title: Follow Their Order
subtitle: Four students. Six stages. One ordering journey.
intro: Same app. Four completely different experiences.

closing.heading: What happens between wanting to order and actually ordering?
closing.body: Four students. Four journeys. But every one of them considered Deliveroo. The question isn't whether students want to order. It's what happens in the gap.

introQuote.text: Ordering a takeaway after an exam… that's an enjoyable meal. It's like a reward. You've earned it.

stages.name: Deciding to order
stages.context: It's Thursday evening. Lectures are done, the library is closed, and tomorrow's assignment can wait. The thought of cooking crosses their mind — and is immediately weighed against the effort.

quote.text: It sort of takes you out from when you're doing, like, work all day. It's just something different that you can focus on.

stages.name: Choosing where & what
stages.context: Three apps open. Delivery fees compared. Menu prices cross-referenced. The minimum order threshold means adding a drink they don't really want to hit £15.

quote.text: I try to make it about £15 to save delivery fees… it makes some changes in my delivery order.

stages.name: Placing the order
stages.context: The calculation is complete. The order is placed. For a brief moment, the optimisation stops and something simpler takes over: anticipation. The fee was managed. The reward is incoming.

stages.name: Waiting for delivery
stages.context: Twenty-five minutes. Time to do something else. The waiting barely registers — the emotional work was in the decision, not the delivery.

stages.name: Eating the food
stages.context: The food arrives exactly as expected. No surprises, no disappointment. The meal is eaten with quiet satisfaction — a small, earned reward after a calculated decision.

stages.name: After the meal
stages.context: Worth it. The math checked out. The fee was saved. The reward was earned. A productive day capped with a strategic indulgence.

quote.text: Whether that's through a productive day or I've gone on a long hike or something. Where you've earned it.

introQuote.text: Oh, it's like an unspoken ritual… We're not meant to call it a 'ritual,' but it definitely is.

stages.name: Deciding to order
stages.context: A friend mentions food. Another one pulls out their phone. The seed is planted. Now it's not about whether to order — it's about being part of the moment.

quote.text: I sat next to my mate, and he was like, 'Oh, I just think I'm going to order something.' And that's all, like, planted a seed in my head.

stages.name: Choosing where & what
stages.context: Everyone has opinions. Different restaurants, different cuisines, different apps. Someone needs to take charge — but deciding for the group means absorbing the blame if it's wrong.

quote.text: Something about takeaways at home even with friends don't feel as social. Whereas if you're out and about together it feels more social for some reason.

stages.name: Placing the order
stages.context: One phone, one account, one subscription splitting the cost. The order goes in and for a moment, the group is aligned: this is their Friday night.

quote.text: I ordered from my account. I have a membership, so I ordered from my account all together and then we split it in like three or four.

stages.name: Waiting for delivery
stages.context: The group shifts to something else — a show, a conversation, a game. The food is coming. That's all that matters right now.

stages.name: Eating the food
stages.context: The food is eaten. The social energy fades. And then, quietly: was that too much? The guilt doesn't come from the food — it comes from eating it alone with your thoughts.

quote.text: When you're going out with friends, it doesn't feel as bad getting a pizza. It feels more like a social thing rather than ordering it to your house and eating it on your own.

stages.name: After the meal
stages.context: It's done. Plates stacked, containers binned. The evening settles into the kind of comfort that only comes from a shared routine. Next Friday, the same thing will happen.

personas.name: The Budget Balancer
personas.tagline: Careful financial management meets occasional treats

introQuote.text: I'm not really the kind of person that likes having subscriptions anyway because I just don't… like money coming out.

stages.name: Deciding to order
stages.context: The internal negotiation begins. Has it been long enough since the last order? Is there food in the fridge? Is this a genuine craving or just convenience? Three conditions must be met before permission is granted.

quote.text: Justified is probably just when it's been a while, if it's something I've been feeling like having, and if it's time to do another grocery shop.

stages.name: Choosing where & what
stages.context: When the answer is yes, there's relief. The guilt of deciding to order is replaced by the comfort of choosing well — finding the right deal, the right restaurant, the right amount. The hard part is over.

stages.name: Placing the order
stages.context: Then the price hits. Delivery fee. Service fee. The total climbs past the comfortable threshold. Subscriptions that promise savings feel like another cost to justify.

stages.name: Waiting for delivery
stages.context: The emotional weight was in the decision. Now there's just time. The waiting is empty — not anxious, not excited, just neutral.

stages.name: Eating the food
stages.context: The food is fine. It's eaten without ceremony. For the Budget Balancer, the drama was in the permission to order, not in the eating. This stage is emotionally flat — and that flatness is the insight.

stages.name: After the meal
stages.context: It's over. The money is spent. And in the quiet aftermath, there's a gentle comfort: it was a while since the last one. It was justified.

quote.text: I've had quite a busy or stressful week as, like, a little reward.

introQuote.text: Usually, when I'm not at home, I'm always ordering off Just Eat, Deliveroo, or UberEats, ordering to the friend's house. About pretty much five times a week.

stages.name: Deciding to order
stages.context: The cereal is gone. The milk is out. The shop is a twenty-minute walk in February rain. The frustration isn't about food — it's about everything else getting in the way of eating.

quote.text: I started noticing that we're running out of cereal, eggs, milk, porridge… I didn't feel like going down to the shop… and I didn't have the time.

stages.name: Choosing where & what
stages.context: The decision is barely a decision. The app is already open. The regular order is right there. The only real choice is which platform has the better deal tonight.

quote.text: I have one main flatmate that I usually coordinate with, like when our lectures are, when we'll go to the gym, and so that we try to eat around the same time.

stages.name: Placing the order
stages.context: The order is placed — from a friend's house, between lectures, coordinated with a flatmate. Delivery isn't an event. It's infrastructure. The positive feeling comes from the routine itself working smoothly.

stages.name: Waiting for delivery
stages.context: The driver can't find the college entrance. The pin drop is wrong. The food sits at a gate while a phone call sorts out what a map couldn't.

quote.text: It's kind of difficult getting the deliveries actually where you want them. Maybe it's just the setup here at Oxford and the different colleges.

stages.name: Eating the food
stages.context: Hot food, couch, winter evening. For a moment, everything makes sense. The convenience was worth it.

quote.text: Definitely winter. I think that's probably what started it… just not wanting to leave the house as much.

stages.name: After the meal
stages.context: Then the bank notification arrives. Ninety pounds this week. Maybe more. The spending is visible, the pattern undeniable. Tomorrow it'll happen again.

quote.text: I'd say about… I don't want to lie… about 90 to 100 quid just alone on food, maybe even more.

cards.quote: It's like a reward. You've earned it.
I ordered from my account all together and then we split it.
I just don't like money coming out.
About pretty much five times a week.
